title: Floquet-Multiple Andreev Reflections
authors: Régis Mélin, Romain Danneau, Morteza Kayyalha
abstract: Floquet theory describes quantum systems governed by time-periodic Hamiltonians, much as Bloch theory describes spatially periodic solids. In voltage-biased multiterminal Josephson junctions, the Josephson relation causes superconducting phase differences to evolve periodically in time, thereby providing an intrinsic Floquet drive. In this Letter, we consider three-terminal Josephson junctions formed on a ballistic two-dimensional normal conductor with a continuum of electronic states. We show that the quartet and higher-order multipair processes yield characteristic Floquet-multiple Andreev reflection (Floquet-MAR) finite-bias conductance and noise resonances that are parameterized by the bias voltage and electrochemical potential. This microscopic picture opens a route toward implementing and probing Floquet-MAR physics in ballistic multiterminal Josephson junctions.
summary: This paper explores how time-periodic driving in multi-terminal Josephson junctions leads to new types of Andreev reflection resonances. It provides a microscopic framework to predict conductance and noise signatures for complex multi-particle transfer processes. The study offers a pathway for engineering and probing Floquet states in superconducting hybrid structures.
main_problem: Investigating the emergence of Floquet-Multiple Andreev Reflection (Floquet-MAR) resonances in the transport properties of multi-terminal Josephson junctions.
main_result: The authors identify that higher-order multipair processes (like quartets and octets) create characteristic conductance and noise resonances, and they prove that the Fano factor for an N-order process scales as 2N.
method: Keldysh Green's function formalism and a diagrammatic expansion using 'snake diagrams' to describe Andreev-tube trajectories.
model_or_system: A three-terminal Josephson junction consisting of a ballistic 2D normal conductor connected to three superconducting leads, driven by voltage-induced periodic phase evolution.
key_observables: Finite-bias conductance, quantum noise cross-correlations, current susceptibility, and the Fano factor.
important_parameters: Bias voltages (VL, VR), electrochemical potential (mu_N), superconducting phases, and the Thouless energy.
main_assumptions: Large-gap approximation, zero-temperature limit, large-system/ballistic limit, and lowest-order perturbation theory in tunneling amplitudes.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows schematic setups and diagrams for quartet and octet transport processes; Figure 2 and 3 use toy models to illustrate the emergence and suppression of resonances at zero bias.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the Floquet-MAR concept, describes the 3-terminal junction model, develops the Keldysh-based microscopic transport theory, analyzes specific higher-order processes (quartets/octets), and concludes with a mathematical derivation of the Fano factor scaling.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 2. Structures of the Andreev pair semiclassical trajectories: (a), (c), (e) and their corresponding energy level diagrams: (b), (d) and (f). On panel (a), (c), and (e), the dark blue lines represent the nonlocal transmission of the Cooper pairs across the 2D conductor (a single line encodes an electron-electron e-e and hole-hole h-h Nambu Green’s functions). (a) corresponds to a Q-quartet simplified semi- classical trajectories diagram, (c) an O-octet simplified semiclassi- cal trajectories diagram and (e) a Q′-quartet simplified semiclassical trajectories diagram together with their corresponding energy level diagram, (b), (d) and (f) respectively.

FIG. 1. Set-up of our study: The square-shape 2D conductor-based 3TJJ of dimension Lx × Ly (a), biased at the voltages (VL, VR, VB), with the superconducting phase variables ϕL(t) = ϕL + 2eVLt/¯h, ϕR(t) = ϕR + 2eVRt/¯h and ϕB at the time t. Top view of the model geometry (b), to obtain a continuum in the weak link, we set the di- mensions of the 2D conductor Lx and Ly to infinity while the super- conducting leads SL, SR and SB are then model as points separated by Rk,l, where k, l = L, R, B. The superconducting leads therefore form multichannel point contacts on the 2D conductor, having the aperture πd2/4 such that d ≫λF, with λF the Fermi wave-length.

FIG. 3. The lowest-order Q-quartet diagram in real space (a), and in the energy representation (b and c). The electrochemical poten- tial fixes the discontinuity in the nonequilibrium Fermi surface at the energy E = µN, see the representation of the filled states in green- color on panel b. A finite-bias resonance in the current susceptibility is obtained in transport if E = µN matches one of the intermediate- state Floquet-MAR energy levels of the quartets, as shown on panel b. Panel c shows a quasi-zero-energy quartet diagram restricted to |ω| ≲ωc. The notations e and h are used for the electron-like and hole-like Nambu Green’s functions, which are equivalently denoted by 1 and 2 in the...

FIG. 4. The Floquet-MAR finite-bias resonances visible in the dimensionless current susceptibility ∂i′(eV,µN).∂µN, as a function of (log(eV), logµN) on the (x,y) axis, for the Q-quartets (a) and (d), the O-octets (b) and (e) and the Q′-quartets (c) and (f). The summation of the microscopic Q, O and Q′ diagrams over the interfaces, is carried out on panels (a), (b) and (c) taking into account nonlocal propagation in the 2D conductor continuum. The contacts form the L×L square-shape geometry in Fig. 1a, with L = 10. Panel (d), (e) and (f) corresponds to maps with averaging over the λF-oscillations in single channels. The colorscale axis is in arbitrary units.
FIG. 1. The O-octet and the Q′-quartet diagrams, see also Fig. 2c and e in the main text.
